meta.title: Fearless Felix
meta.author: Rus Buyok
meta.focusQuestion: How did Felix Baumgartner's personality, accomplishments, and influence change the world of extreme sports and scientific research?

sections.title: The Jump Seen Around the World
paragraphs: On October 14, 2012, millions of people around the world sat glued to their screens. They were watching something no one had ever seen before. A man was about to jump from the edge of space.

The man's name was Felix Baumgartner. He stood inside a tiny capsule hanging from a massive balloon nearly 24 miles above Earth. Below him, the curve of the planet glowed blue and white against the blackness of space.

When Felix stepped out of the capsule and jumped, he fell faster than anyone had ever fallen. He broke the speed of sound — the speed that only jet fighters and bullets had reached. People cheered in living rooms, sports bars, and city squares across the globe.
The jump was seen around the world — on television, on the internet, and on phones. But who was this man who jumped from space? And how did he do it?

sections.title: Who Is Felix?
paragraphs: Felix Baumgartner was born on April 20, 1969, in Salzburg, Austria. Salzburg is a beautiful city surrounded by mountains. As a child, Felix loved to climb trees, scale walls, and explore anything high.
His father introduced him to parachuting when Felix was just 16 years old. Felix took to the sky naturally. He was not afraid of heights. In fact, he loved them.
Felix joined the Austrian military, where he continued to parachute and train as an athlete. After his military service, he became a professional skydiver and BASE jumper.

A BASE jumper leaps from fixed objects — Buildings, Antennas, Spans (bridges), and Earth (cliffs) — and uses a parachute to land safely. It is one of the most dangerous sports in the world. But Felix was not just any BASE jumper. He was one who always looked for the next big challenge.

sections.title: First World Record
paragraphs: In 1999, Felix made his first world-record jump. He leaped from the Petronas Towers in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. At 1,483 feet, the Petronas Towers were the tallest buildings in the world at the time.

Felix stepped off the edge of the tower and plunged into free fall. The wind roared past him as the ground rushed up. At just the right moment, he deployed his parachute and floated safely to the ground.
This jump made Felix famous. People could not believe that someone had jumped from the tallest buildings in the world. But Felix was just getting started.

sections.title: More Records
paragraphs: In 2003, Felix made another daring jump. He leaped from the outstretched hand of the Christ the Redeemer statue in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. The statue stands 125 feet above the ground on top of a mountain. Felix's jump from the statue was the lowest BASE jump ever recorded.

In 2004, Felix did something no one had done before. He used a special carbon-fiber wing to fly across the English Channel from Dover, England, to Calais, France. He was the first person to fly across the Channel using only a wing attached to his body. The crossing took about 6 minutes.
Each jump brought Felix more attention. But he dreamed of something even bigger — a jump from the very edge of space.

sections.title: The Stratosphere Project
paragraphs: In 2010, Red Bull announced the Stratosphere Project. The goal was to send Felix Baumgartner to the edge of space in a balloon so he could jump from the stratosphere. The stratosphere is a layer of Earth's atmosphere roughly 23 miles above the surface.

A team of scientists, engineers, and doctors came together to plan the jump. They knew that at such extreme altitudes, the air is almost nonexistent. Without protection, a person could not breathe. The low pressure could even cause the blood to boil.

The team needed to solve many problems. How could they get Felix to the stratosphere? How could they keep him alive during the climb? How could he survive the extreme cold and the high speeds of free fall? It would take two years of planning and testing.

sections.title: The Suit
paragraphs: The most important piece of equipment was Felix's pressurized suit. The suit was specially designed to keep Felix alive at extreme altitudes. It was like a miniature spaceship that he wore on his body.

The suit had to maintain the right pressure around Felix's body. It kept him warm in temperatures as low as 100 degrees below zero. It supplied him with oxygen to breathe. If the suit failed at high altitude, Felix could die in seconds.

The suit also had to be flexible enough for Felix to move during free fall. It had to bend at the knees, elbows, and waist. Engineers worked for months to design a suit that could do all of these things. Specialized sensors inside the suit tracked Felix's heartbeat, breathing, and body temperature throughout the jump.

sections.title: Balloon and Capsule
paragraphs: To reach the stratosphere, the team built an enormous helium balloon. The balloon was so large that it could lift the capsule and Felix nearly 128,000 feet into the air — almost 24 miles above Earth's surface.

Felix would ride inside a small pressurized capsule during the ascent. The capsule was just large enough for him to stand inside. It had windows so he could see out and instruments to monitor the balloon's altitude.

The capsule was sealed and pressurized so Felix could breathe during the long climb. It took about two and a half hours to reach the target altitude. During that time, Felix had to stay calm inside the small space. People with claustrophobia — a fear of small, enclosed spaces — would never be able to make the trip.

sections.title: Test Jumps
paragraphs: Before the big jump, Felix had to make several test jumps to make sure everything worked. Each test jump was higher than the last one.

During the first test jumps, Felix jumped from 7,000 feet, then 15,000 feet, then much higher. He tested the suit, the capsule, and the balloon at real altitudes. Each jump gave the team more data about how the equipment performed.
Some test jumps revealed problems. The suit fogged up. The capsule door was hard to open. Each problem had to be fixed before the team could try the real jump.

Felix's discipline and hard work during the testing phase were just as important as his bravery. He followed every procedure carefully. He studied the data from each jump. He worked with the engineers to solve every problem.

sections.title: The Big Jump
paragraphs: On October 14, 2012, the team launched the balloon from a site in Roswell, New Mexico. The same area where the first UFO sighting had been reported decades earlier was now the site of a very real scientific achievement.
After about two and a half hours, the capsule reached its target altitude: 128,100 feet — nearly 24 miles above Earth. This was more than 8,000 feet higher than the previous altitude record.
Felix stood inside the capsule. He looked out the window at the curve of the Earth below. He took a deep breath. Then he opened the door and stepped out.
For a few seconds, Felix just floated. The air was so thin at that altitude that it could not slow him down. Then gravity pulled him into a steep dive. He fell faster and faster.

Felix reached a top speed of 833.9 miles per hour. That was 834 mph — faster than the speed of sound, which is 761 mph. He was the first person to break the speed of sound outside of a vehicle. The sonic boom could be heard on the ground below.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: After about four minutes of free fall, Felix deployed his parachute. He floated gently to the desert floor below, landing safely. People around the world erupted in cheers.

Felix had set two new records. He had jumped from the highest altitude ever — 128,100 feet. He had also fallen at the fastest speed ever recorded — 833.9 mph. He was the first person to break the speed of sound without a vehicle.
"Sometimes you have to go up really high to understand how small you are," Felix said after the jump.

But Felix's jump was about more than records. The data from the Stratosphere Project helped scientists learn about how humans and equipment perform at extreme altitudes. This information could help improve safety for astronauts, pilots, and future space travelers. Felix Baumgartner had not only made history — he had helped push the boundaries of what humans can do.

vocabulary.definition: the height of an object above the ground or above sea level
vocabulary.sentence: The stratosphere is a layer of Earth's atmosphere roughly 23 miles above the surface — an extreme altitude.

options: the depth of an object below sea level
the speed at which an object travels
the height of an object above the ground or sea level
the distance between two cities

vocabulary.definition: a person who jumps from fixed objects such as buildings, antennas, spans, or earth using a parachute
vocabulary.sentence: A BASE jumper leaps from fixed objects and uses a parachute to land safely.

options: a pilot who flies small aircraft
a person who leaps from fixed objects using a parachute
a climber who scales tall mountains
a skydiver who jumps from commercial airplanes

vocabulary.definition: a small enclosed compartment or container, especially one used for space travel
vocabulary.sentence: Felix would ride inside a small pressurized capsule during the ascent.

options: a large vehicle used for transportation
a small enclosed compartment used for travel
a type of parachute used by skydivers
a helmet worn by pilots

vocabulary.definition: a rapid descent through the air before a parachute is opened
vocabulary.sentence: For a few seconds, Felix just floated in free fall before gravity pulled him into a steep dive.

options: a controlled glide using wings
a slow float with a parachute
a rapid descent before a parachute opens
a gentle landing on soft ground

vocabulary.definition: sealed and maintained at a specific air pressure to protect occupants
vocabulary.sentence: The capsule was sealed and pressurized so Felix could breathe during the long climb.

options: open to the outside air
ventilated for comfort
heated to a high temperature
sealed and maintained at a specific air pressure

vocabulary.definition: the speed at which sound waves travel through air, approximately 761 miles per hour at sea level
vocabulary.sentence: Felix was the first person to break the speed of sound outside of a vehicle.

options: the speed at which sound waves travel through air
the fastest speed a human can travel
the force of gravity pulling objects downward
the velocity of light in a vacuum

vocabulary.definition: an extreme fear of small or enclosed spaces
vocabulary.sentence: People with claustrophobia would never be able to make the trip inside the small capsule.

options: a fear of extreme heights
a fear of fast speeds
an extreme fear of small or enclosed spaces
a fear of dark places

vocabulary.definition: opening and releasing a parachute so it fills with air
vocabulary.sentence: At just the right moment, he deployed his parachute and floated safely to the ground.

options: collecting and storing equipment
folding carefully into a small space
releasing and opening so it spreads out
attaching something to a vehicle

vocabulary.definition: the ability to control one's behavior and follow a strict set of rules or procedures
vocabulary.sentence: Felix's discipline and hard work during the testing phase were just as important as his bravery.

options: a strict punishment for breaking rules
a natural talent for sports and athletics
the ability to control oneself and follow procedures
a method of training animals

vocabulary.definition: devices that detect and measure changes in physical conditions
vocabulary.sentence: Specialized sensors inside the suit tracked Felix's heartbeat, breathing, and body temperature.

options: devices that detect and measure physical changes
buttons used to start machines
switches that turn equipment on and off
tools used to repair broken equipment

vocabulary.definition: the sport of jumping from an aircraft and free falling before deploying a parachute
vocabulary.sentence: After his military service, Felix became a professional skydiver and BASE jumper.

options: the sport of climbing tall mountains
the sport of swimming in deep water
the sport of riding in hot air balloons
jumping from an aircraft and free falling with a parachute

vocabulary.definition: designed or developed for a particular purpose; custom-made
vocabulary.sentence: The suit was specially designed — a specialized piece of equipment — to keep Felix alive at extreme altitudes.

options: ordinary and used by everyone
designed for a particular purpose
general-purpose and widely available
cheap and easy to replace

questions.question: What record did Felix set in 1999?
questions.evidence: In 1999, Felix made his first world-record jump. He leaped from the Petronas Towers in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. At 1,483 feet, the Petronas Towers were the tallest buildings in the world at the time.
choices: He jumped from the world's tallest buildings, the Petronas Towers.
He flew across the English Channel with a carbon-fiber wing.
He leaped from the hand of the Christ the Redeemer statue in Brazil.
He jumped from the stratosphere, nearly 24 miles above the Earth.
questions.explanation: In 1999, Felix jumped from the Petronas Towers in Malaysia, which at 1,483 feet were the tallest buildings in the world at that time.

questions.question: What was the primary purpose of the Stratosphere Project?
questions.evidence: In 2010, Red Bull announced the Stratosphere Project. The goal was to send Felix Baumgartner to the edge of space in a balloon so he could jump from the stratosphere.
choices: To study weather patterns in the stratosphere
To test new parachute designs for military use
To send Felix to the edge of space so he could jump from the stratosphere
To set a new hot air balloon altitude record
questions.explanation: The Stratosphere Project's goal was to send Felix to the edge of space in a balloon so he could jump from the stratosphere — about 23 miles above Earth.

questions.question: What was the purpose of the pressurized capsule?
questions.evidence: The capsule was sealed and pressurized so Felix could breathe during the long climb. It took about two and a half hours to reach the target altitude.
choices: To store equipment during the balloon's ascent
To film the jump for a worldwide television broadcast
To communicate with mission control on the ground below
To let Felix breathe during the long climb to the stratosphere
questions.explanation: The pressurized capsule carried Felix during the two-and-a-half-hour ascent. It was sealed so he could breathe at altitudes where the air is almost nonexistent.

questions.question: What made Felix's jump famous around the world?
questions.evidence: When Felix stepped out of the capsule and jumped, he fell faster than anyone had ever fallen. He broke the speed of sound. ... The jump was seen around the world — on television, on the internet, and on phones.
choices: It was broadcast live as Felix broke the speed of sound during his fall.
He was the first person to ever use a parachute during a jump.
He set a new record for the longest parachute ride in history.
He jumped from a tall building for the very first time in history.
questions.explanation: The jump was seen around the world because it was broadcast live on television, the internet, and phones — and Felix broke the speed of sound during his fall.

questions.question: What record did Felix break during his free fall?
questions.evidence: Felix reached a top speed of 833.9 miles per hour. ... He was the first person to break the speed of sound outside of a vehicle.
choices: He set a speed record of 834 mph — first to break the speed of sound outside a vehicle.
He set a new free fall time record of over ten minutes before deploying his parachute.
He set a new altitude record by jumping from 128,100 feet above the Earth.
He set a record for the longest distance ever covered during a single free fall.
questions.explanation: Felix reached 833.9 mph during his free fall, becoming the first person to break the speed of sound (761 mph) outside of a vehicle.

questions.question: Why did the suit need to be pressurized?
questions.evidence: The suit had to maintain the right pressure around Felix's body. ... If the suit failed at high altitude, Felix could die in seconds.
choices: To make Felix look professional on camera
To protect him from extreme cold and low air pressure
To make the high-altitude jump more comfortable for Felix
To help Felix steer during free fall
questions.explanation: At extreme altitudes, the air pressure is so low that blood can boil and a person cannot breathe. The pressurized suit protected Felix from the extreme cold (100 degrees below zero) and maintained the right pressure for survival.

questions.question: How many test jumps did Felix make before the big jump?
questions.evidence: Before the big jump, Felix had to make several test jumps to make sure everything worked. Each test jump was higher than the last one. ... During the first test jumps, Felix jumped from 7,000 feet, then 15,000 feet, then much higher.
choices: He completed several test jumps, each higher than the last
He made exactly three test jumps from 7,000, 15,000, and 30,000 feet
He made just one test jump from 7,000 feet
He practiced in a large swimming pool to simulate landing before real jumps
questions.explanation: Felix made several test jumps at increasing altitudes — 7,000 feet, then 15,000 feet, then much higher — to test all the equipment before the real jump.

questions.question: What sport did Felix do as a teenager that helped prepare him for his future career?
questions.evidence: His father introduced him to parachuting when Felix was just 16 years old. Felix took to the sky naturally. He was not afraid of heights.
choices: He competed in downhill skiing competitions.
His father introduced him to parachuting at age 16.
He practiced rock climbing in the Austrian mountains.
He trained as a pilot in the Austrian military.
questions.explanation: Felix's father introduced him to parachuting when he was only 16. This early experience in the sky gave him the foundation for his career as a professional skydiver and BASE jumper.

questions.question: Which of Felix's accomplishments best shows his discipline, not just his bravery?
questions.evidence: Felix's discipline and hard work during the testing phase were just as important as his bravery. He followed every procedure carefully. He studied the data from each jump. He worked with the engineers to solve every problem.
choices: Jumping from the Petronas Towers in 1999
Flying across the English Channel with a wing
Carefully following procedures and studying data during months of test jumps
Jumping from Christ the Redeemer in Brazil
questions.explanation: The text explicitly says Felix's 'discipline and hard work during the testing phase were just as important as his bravery.' He followed procedures, studied data, and worked with engineers — all signs of discipline, not just daring.

questions.question: How did Felix's jump contribute to science?
questions.evidence: The data from the Stratosphere Project helped scientists learn about how humans and equipment perform at extreme altitudes. This information could help improve safety for astronauts, pilots, and future space travelers.
choices: The data from his jump showed that humans cannot survive at extreme altitudes even with specialized equipment.
It showed that parachutes are no longer needed for high-altitude jumps
It provided data on human performance at extreme altitudes for space travel safety
It proved that balloons can travel faster than jet aircraft at high altitudes.
questions.explanation: The Stratosphere Project data helped scientists understand how humans and equipment perform at extreme altitudes. This information could improve safety for astronauts, pilots, and future space travelers.

questions.question: What do you think was Felix Baumgartner's most important accomplishment — the altitude record, breaking the speed of sound, or contributing to scientific research? Use evidence from the text to support your answer.
questions.explanation: Think about all of Felix's accomplishments. Which one had the biggest impact — on history, on science, or on future generations?
opinionPrompts: What are some of Felix Baumgartner's major accomplishments mentioned in the text?
Which accomplishment — the altitude record, breaking the speed of sound, or the scientific data — do you think was most important and why?
What evidence from the text supports your answer?

characterAnalysis.quote: "Sometimes you have to go up really high to understand how small you are." —Felix Baumgartner
characterAnalysis.quotePrompt: How does this quote reflect Felix's personality? How did his personality lead to his accomplishments? How did his accomplishments influence the world?

Personality.prompt: What qualities made Felix Baumgartner unique?
items: Fearless — not afraid of heights or extreme danger
Disciplined — followed every procedure during testing
Hardworking — spent two years planning and testing
Daring — always looked for the next big challenge
Meticulous planner — studied data and solved every problem before jumping
First BASE jump from the Petronas Towers (1,483 ft) — the tallest buildings in the world in 1999
Lowest BASE jump ever from Christ the Redeemer statue in Rio de Janeiro (125 ft)
First person to fly across the English Channel with a carbon-fiber wing (2004)
Broke altitude record: jumped from 128,100 feet — nearly 24 miles above Earth
Broke speed record: reached 834 mph in free fall — first to break the speed of sound outside a vehicle

Influence.prompt: How did Felix Baumgartner affect the world?
items: Inspired scientific research on high-altitude and space travel safety
Proved humans can survive extreme altitudes with proper equipment
Advanced understanding of free fall at extreme altitudes
Data from the Stratosphere Project could help improve safety for astronauts, pilots, and future space travelers
Inspired millions of people around the world by showing what courage and discipline can achieve

prompts.mainIdea: What is the main idea of 'Fearless Felix'? What details from the text support this main idea?
prompts.beginning: How did Felix's early life and personality prepare him for his future as a BASE jumper and record-setter?
prompts.middle: What steps did Felix and his team take to prepare for the Stratosphere Project? What equipment did they need?
prompts.end: What records did Felix break during the big jump? How did his accomplishment help science and future space travelers?

keyDetails: Felix was born in Austria and introduced to parachuting at age 16 by his father.
He became a professional skydiver and BASE jumper — one of the most dangerous sports.
In 1999, he set his first world record by jumping from the Petronas Towers (1,483 ft).
He made more records: lowest BASE jump from Christ the Redeemer, first to fly across the English Channel with a wing.
The Stratosphere Project required a pressurized suit, an enormous helium balloon, a small capsule, and years of planning.
Felix made several test jumps at increasing altitudes to make sure all the equipment worked.
On October 14, 2012, Felix jumped from 128,100 feet and reached 833.9 mph — breaking the speed of sound.
The data from his jump could help improve safety for astronauts and future space travelers.

grammar.title: Complex Sentences
grammar.explanation: A complex sentence has one independent clause (a complete thought that can stand alone) and at least one dependent clause (an incomplete thought that cannot stand alone). The clauses are joined by a subordinating conjunction such as because, although, when, since, if, while, after, before, until, or unless.
rules: A complex sentence = one independent clause + one or more dependent clauses
Subordinating conjunctions connect the dependent clause to the independent clause: because, although, when, since, if, while, after, before, until, unless, even though, as soon as
When the dependent clause comes first, use a comma after it: 'Although he was afraid, he jumped.'
When the independent clause comes first, no comma is usually needed: 'He jumped although he was afraid.'

Example: 'When Felix stepped out of the capsule, he fell faster than anyone had ever fallen.' — Dependent clause ('When Felix stepped out of the capsule') + Independent clause ('he fell faster than anyone had ever fallen')

wordBuilding.base: Dependent clause: Although the capsule was small
wordBuilding.answer: Although the capsule was small, Felix stood inside it during the two-and-a-half-hour ascent.

wordBuilding.base: Dependent clause: Because the air was so thin at that altitude
wordBuilding.answer: Because the air was so thin at that altitude, it could not slow him down.

wordBuilding.base: Dependent clause: If the suit failed at high altitude
wordBuilding.answer: If the suit failed at high altitude, Felix could die in seconds.

wordBuilding.base: Dependent clause: After about four minutes of free fall
wordBuilding.answer: After about four minutes of free fall, Felix deployed his parachute.

wordBuilding.base: Dependent clause: Since Felix was not afraid of heights
wordBuilding.answer: Since Felix was not afraid of heights, he took to the sky naturally.

exercises.sentence: The balloon was so large that it could lift the capsule nearly 128,000 feet into the air.
exercises.explanation: This is a complex sentence. Independent clause: 'The balloon was so large.' Dependent clause: 'that it could lift the capsule nearly 128,000 feet into the air.' The subordinating conjunction is 'that.'

exercises.sentence: Felix was born in Salzburg, Austria.
exercises.explanation: This is a simple sentence. It has only one independent clause and no dependent clause.

exercises.sentence: Before the big jump, Felix made several test jumps.
exercises.explanation: This is a complex sentence. Dependent clause: 'Before the big jump.' Independent clause: 'Felix made several test jumps.' The subordinating conjunction is 'Before.'

exercises.sentence: Felix and his team worked together for two years.
exercises.explanation: This is a simple sentence. It has one subject (Felix and his team) and one predicate, with no dependent clause.

wordWork.title: Reading Numbers Aloud
wordWork.explanation: When we read large numbers aloud, we use place-value words like thousand, million, and hundred. Commas help us group digits into chunks. In '128,100,' the comma separates the thousands group (128) from the ones group (100). We read it as 'one hundred twenty-eight thousand, one hundred.'
rules: Read each group of three digits (separated by commas) as its own mini-number, then add the place-value word
833.9 → 'eight hundred thirty-three point nine' (for decimals, read each digit after the point individually)

jumpAroundWorld.title: A Jump Seen Around the World
jumpAroundWorld.content: On October 14, 2012, MILLIONS watched Felix jump from the edge of space! He broke the SPEED OF SOUND — only jet fighters and bullets had gone that fast. The whole world was watching!

jumpAroundWorld.title: Speed of Sound
jumpAroundWorld.content: The speed of sound = about 761 mph. Felix went FASTER than that — 833.9 mph! He was the first person to do this without a vehicle.

whoIsFelix.title: BASE Jumper & Skydiving
whoIsFelix.content: BASE = Buildings, Antennas, Spans, Earth. A BASE jumper leaps from these fixed objects. Felix started parachuting at just 16 years old!

whoIsFelix.title: Character Trait: Fearless
whoIsFelix.content: Felix was NOT afraid of heights — he LOVED them. This is a key personality trait that made him suited for extreme sports.

firstWorldRecord.title: Deploying & Free Fall
firstWorldRecord.content: Deploy = to release/open. Felix DEPLOYED his parachute after free fall (a rapid descent before the parachute opens). FREE FALL = falling fast before pulling the chute!

firstWorldRecord.title: Detail: Petronas Towers
firstWorldRecord.content: The Petronas Towers were 1,483 feet — the TALLEST buildings in the world in 1999. Imagine stepping off the top!

moreRecords.title: Felix's Growing Accomplishments
moreRecords.content: 1999: Petronas Towers. 2003: Christ the Redeemer (LOWEST BASE jump). 2004: English Channel with a WING! Each jump was more daring than the last.

stratosphereProject.title: Altitude
stratosphereProject.content: Altitude = height above the ground. The STRATOSPHERE is about 23 miles up — EXTREME altitude! Air is so thin you can't breathe, and blood can boil!

stratosphereProject.title: Main Idea & Details
stratosphereProject.content: MAIN IDEA: The Stratosphere Project was a huge challenge. DETAILS: They needed to solve problems about breathing, pressure, temperature, and speed. It took 2 YEARS of planning!

theSuit.title: Pressurized, Sensors, Specialized
theSuit.content: PRESSURIZED = sealed at the right air pressure. SENSORS = detectors that track heartbeat and breathing. SPECIALIZED = custom-designed for one specific purpose. The suit was like a mini SPACESHIP!

balloonAndCapsule.title: Capsule & Claustrophobia
balloonAndCapsule.content: A CAPSULE is a small enclosed compartment. Felix stood inside it for 2.5 HOURS! CLAUSTRPHOBIA = fear of small spaces. Could YOU stand in a tiny box for hours?

balloonAndCapsule.title: Complex Sentence
balloonAndCapsule.content: 'If the suit failed at high altitude, Felix could die in seconds.' — Dependent clause (If...) + Independent clause (Felix could die...). Subordinating conjunction = IF.

testJumps.title: Discipline
testJumps.content: DISCIPLINE = self-control and following procedures. The text says Felix's DISCIPLINE was just as important as his BRAVERY! He studied data and solved every problem.

testJumps.title: Summarize: The Testing Process
testJumps.content: Felix made SEVERAL test jumps, each HIGHER than the last: 7,000 ft → 15,000 ft → much higher. Problems found: suit fogged up, capsule door hard to open. Each problem was FIXED before the big jump.

theBigJump.title: The Record-Breaking Jump
theBigJump.content: ALTITUDE: 128,100 feet (24 miles!) — that's 8,000+ feet higher than the old record. SPEED: 833.9 mph — broke the speed of sound (761 mph). First person EVER to do this without a vehicle!

theBigJump.title: Free Fall & Speed of Sound
theBigJump.content: FREE FALL: rapid descent before the parachute opens. Felix was in free fall for about 4 MINUTES! SPEED OF SOUND = 761 mph. Felix went 833.9 — he broke it!

conclusion.title: Felix's Legacy
conclusion.content: Felix set TWO records: highest altitude jump AND fastest free fall speed! But his jump was about MORE than records — the data could help astronauts, pilots, and future SPACE TRAVELERS stay safe!
